frontmatter.title: Agent Foundations
frontmatter.summary: An introduction to agent foundations — embedded agency, reflective stability, Löb's obstacle, and the prescriptive vs descriptive research agendas for understanding agents.
frontmatter.teachingGuide:
* 1 hour of introductory lecture on the bird’s eye view picture & motivation of agent foundations  
* 2.5 hours of reading \+ discussion  
  * Format: We separate into fundamental reading vs readings on specific topics, each person reads a fundamental reading+ readings on one of the topics, then discuss with others who’ve read different topics  
  * Fundamental reading:  
    * Embedded agency  
    * Why agent foundations  
    * General purpose search  
    * Reflective consistent degree of freedom  
  * Topics:  
    * Consequentialist foundations (coherence & complete class theorems)  
    * Lob’s theorem and tiling agent  
    * Logical induction  
    * Decision theory  
    * Optimization and thermodynamics  
    * Descriptive agent foundations  
* 1 hour of lecture by Cole Wyeth on [reflective oracles and nonrealizability](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1lV82lyZeY1nLMu-LMd4WlhF4D-iL-cer/view?usp=sharing)  
* 1 hour of exercises on [Lob’s theorem, complete class and information theory](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1eQE_i8vImFuSj-bsKpMtsNr3fe1xUXQ3/view?usp=sharing)  
* 1 hour of lecture by Aram Ebtekar on [decision theory](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1lV82lyZeY1nLMu-LMd4WlhF4D-iL-cer/view?usp=sharing)

If running this module in person, have participants each read a different topic reading beforehand, then form small groups where each member has read a different topic. Have them discuss how their respective topics relate to each other and to the core problems in agent foundations. The following are prompts for small group discussions, each connecting two topic readings.

## Cross Pollinations {#cross-pollinations}

### Logical Induction × Löb's Theorem & Tiling Agents

The self-trust property (Section 4.12 of [logical induction](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1609.03543)) says that a logical inductor's current credence in any sentence is a weighted average of its expected future credences — it defers to its future self without needing to witness the reasoning trace of its future self. The tiling agent's core desideratum is similar: a parent agent should be able to trust that its successor will act well without inspecting individual actions. Both involve a system placing trust in a future or downstream version of itself. Discuss the relationship between these two notions of self-trust.

### Löb's Theorem × Decision Theory

Löb's theorem constrains what a proof system can prove about its own soundness. Reflective consistency requires that an agent would endorse its own decision procedure if it could choose one from scratch. FDT requires reasoning about the logical consequences of being "the kind of agent that runs this procedure", which involves the agent reasoning about its own source code. Discuss what these three ideas have in common regarding self-reference.

### Consequentialist Foundations × Optimization & Thermodynamics

The ground of optimization and generalized heat engine describe optimization as a process that produces low-entropy (near-deterministic) outputs by moving uncertainty elsewhere — analogous to a heat engine exploiting a temperature gradient. The coherence theorems describe rational agency as EU maximization over uncertain outcomes. Both frameworks are trying to characterize what it means to pursue goals under uncertainty. Discuss how these two pictures of goal-directed behavior relate to each other. Can the thermodynamic picture offer a "measuring stick of utility"?

### Logical Induction x Consequentialist Foundations

Logical uncertainty might seem disentangled from empirical uncertainty, but are there cases where logical uncertainty may be entangled with empirical uncertainty? (E.g. Does the output of a program (whose source code you already know) ever lead you to update on empirical facts about the world? Do new observations ever reduce your uncertainty about logical facts?)

### Logical Induction × Decision Theory

Standard decision theory assumes agents have well-defined credences over empirical states of the world, but many core decision-theoretic scenarios involve uncertainty over logical facts: whether a particular program halts, what a Newcomb predictor has computed, what other instances of one's own decision procedure will output. Discuss what decision-theoretic problems relate to problems of logical uncertainty, and how the logical induction framework bears on them.

### Optimization & Thermodynamics × Descriptive Agent Foundations

The generalized heat engine describes optimization as moving uncertainty around — producing low-entropy outputs by exploiting entropy gradients elsewhere — with the second law as a hard physical constraint on what is possible. Selection theorems ask what agent type signatures are selected for by optimization processes like evolution or ML training, treating the environment as the external constraint. Discuss how these two perspectives relate to each other, e.g. what constraints might thermodynamics impose on the selection environment?

### Consequentialist Foundations × Descriptive Agent Foundations

The complete class theorem is representational: it shows that any agent avoiding dominated strategies *can be described as* an EU maximizer, without making claims about its internal structure. Descriptive agent foundations and selection theorems take the complementary direction, asking what agents actually are and what pressures give rise to them. Discuss the relationship between the representational and mechanistic perspectives on agency.

### Consequentialist Foundations × Logical Induction

The Dutch book argument underlying coherence theorems assumes the agent can assign the correct probability to any bet — but this implicitly presupposes logical omniscience, since computing expected utilities requires knowing the logical consequences of one's beliefs. The logical induction criterion is motivated by an analogous "no polynomial-time Dutch book" argument, extended to cover logical uncertainty. Discuss how logical uncertainty interacts with the
learningOutcomes: Explain why alignment requires reasoning about superintelligent agents in advance, before such systems exist.
Explain Goodhart's law and why a good theory of agency requires concepts that remain meaningful under optimization pressure.
Explain reflective stability and why safety properties that are not invariant under self-modification provide weaker alignment guarantees.
Explain the distinction between dualistic and embedded agents, and why the embedded picture raises qualitatively new challenges.

Identify and explain the core subproblems of embedded agency: reasoning about environments larger than the agent, reasoning about environments containing copies of the agent, and reasoning about self-improvement and successor construction.
Explain the Löbian obstacle and why a consistent formal system cannot vouch for the soundness of an equally powerful successor.

Explain what descriptive agent foundations is trying to do: characterize what agents arising in the physical world actually look like, starting from properties of the world (selection pressures, modularity, computational constraints) rather than from idealized behavior.
frontmatter.notesForFutureIterations:
* More focused scope which focuses on the big picture  
* Maybe develop self-sufficient lecture notes that tie together different topics  
* Develop a list of "what we should take away from this day"  
* More time for exercises and cross pollination discussions  
* Maybe it would be fruitful to have a doom day/a day that explains and motivates the considerations behind agent foundations research. E.g. [security mindset](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/8gqrbnW758qjHFTrH/security-mindset-and-ordinary-paranoia), or something similar to degrees of freedom from Jeremy’s talk

frontmatter.title: World Models
frontmatter.summary: Theoretical and practical foundations of world models — from control theory, neuroscience, and RL framings (POMDPs, belief MDPs, transducers) to modern systems (Ha & Schmidhuber, Dreamer, Genie, JEPA) and the role of symmetries in building abstractions.
frontmatter.teachingGuide:
* The current rendition was done as follows:  
  * Morning lecture about general introduction to world models (1hr).  
  * Discussion (1hr)  
  * Lunch break.  
  * Afternoon lecture on formal approaches to world modelling \+ discussion (1.5hr).  
  * Break.  
  * Late afternoon session on abstractions in world models (1hr).

**Notes**

* The current rendition was heavily focused on lectures. There may be reasons to change this in the future, including exercises and more discussion.  
* The overall structure of the session I think is adequate: first providing a general overview, then going into formalisations of world modeling, and then connecting it with the idea of abstractions from C.4. However, the delivery of these contents may be made less based on lectures and more based on discussions and exercises.
learningOutcomes: Obtain a theoretical understanding of how world models are understood and used in AI, and also in other disciplines including control theory, neuroscience, and cognitive science.
Develop a conceptual understanding of how world models are used in modern AI systems, including Ha & Schmidhuber, Dreamer, Genie, and JEPA.
Have a critical understanding of why world models matter — how agents can use world models, and what can they do with world models that they couldn't do without them.

Become familiar with the notion of transducer / stochastic automata as a general architecture of world models, and how it generalises various kinds of world models in reinforcement learning (e.g. POMDPs and beliefs MDPs).
Reason about how agents can build abstractions as coarse-grainings of world models.
Understand the role of symmetries in the construction of abstractions in world models.
frontmatter.notesForFutureIterations:
* The content for the morning session was prepared a bit in a rush. The basic content is going in a good direction, but future iterations may want to further develop and polish the notes, and the slides.  
* The slides for the afternoon sessions is good, but are a bit too centered on my own work (Fernando). It would be good to expand the content to be more inclusive and include more perspectives.  
* This iteration didn’t include exercises just because of lack of time to properly prepare them. It would be useful for the next iteration to prepare a good set of exercises.

frontmatter.title: Debate
frontmatter.summary: AI safety via debate as scalable oversight — proof checkers, the PSPACE debate protocol, cross-examination up to NEXP, obfuscated arguments, prover-estimator debate, and the AISI safety case.
frontmatter.teachingGuide:
Here we outline how the material was taught in-person in April:

|  | Schedule 28.04. Debate |
| :---- | ----- |
| 10:00 | Fun exercise “Wrong but convincing proofs”. In this exercise, a series of wrong proofs is presented and the students are asked to find the mistake in the proof. The students basically take the role of “Bob” who points out the flaws in Alice’s proofs.This gives an intuition that even mathematical proofs can sound very convincing, and finding a mistake as a human judge is often non-trivial. I will produce 4 such proofs in increasing difficulty. It also leads up to the argument that   [Wrong\_Proofs.pdf](https://drive.google.com/file/d/16jEJXkg5F590kHyvTzub5zV5A51RrZE5/view?usp=drive_link) The first three proofs are good, the fourth is a bit too obviously wrong, the fifth is too difficult for most students. Potentially replace the last two with better ones.  |
| 10:30 | This part is an In-depth explanation of the main formalisms of debate and cross-examination. This includes Formal Definition of the setup Proof sketches for the hardness-reduction for PSPACE and NEXP respectively. These can be sketched at the whiteboard with computation table of a Turing machine A few exercises  This is based on this (still evolving) document: [Debate\_Proofs\_and Exercises.pdf](https://drive.google.com/file/d/16A0hSaMEaBNUPYNvv6c2B5kp1lYfG550/view?usp=drive_link) To illustrate the power of the debate setup, this section also includes an example  |
| 12:30 | **Lunch Break** |
| 13:30 | Splitting the group into 3 to read some critiques of AI safety via debate. [Critique by Marius Hobbhahn with replies by Rohin Shah](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/kguLeJTt6LnGuYX4E/the-limits-of-ai-safety-via-debate)  [Critique by Michael Cohen with replies by Paul Christiano:](https://www.alignmentforum.org/posts/L3QDs6of4Rb2TgpRD/the-ai-debate-debate)  [Obfuscated Arguments by Beth Barnes](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/PJLABqQ962hZEqhdB/debate-update-obfuscated-arguments-problem) Include also the discussion in the comments under the posts\!  **Guided Discussion:** What have people criticised about AISvD? Which criticisms are sustained and which have been conceded in the comments? Which criticisms do you think are the strongest and the hardest to rectify?  |
| 14:30 | **Pause** |
| 15:30 | Reading session: [The AI safety case of the AISI](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.03989) as preparation for the later talk. The idea is that the students already know the structure of the talk: The general idea The many assumptions that the safety case makes The path towards automated research and ultimately alignment The goal is that the students can follow the talk and already ask questions while it is given. |
| 16:00 | Talk **“AISI Debate Safety Case”.** This presentation explains the AISI’s idea of the safety case based on AI safety via debate. |
| 17:00 | **End** |
learningOutcomes: Understand the difference between syntactic and semantic problems and why human judgment is necessary for a reward signal

Understand the basic idea behind AI Safety via Debate as a Scalable Oversight Technique: If a task can be recursively decomposed in a debate, this technique allows us to extend a training signal on reasoning tasks into a regime where AI systems are smarter than humans
Get an intuition about debate and cross-examination, and why they so drastically reduce the need for human oversight
Understand the limitations inherent to the setup, and some of the open problems/counterarguments
Are familiar with the UK AISI safety case, and are able to defend/critique it

Most important Take-Away: Debate is a form of scalable oversight: it seeks to extend a reward signal for honest solutions to difficult problems while reducing the amount of human judgment needed by an exponential factor.
frontmatter.notesForFutureIterations:
* The idea behind debate needs further motivation. Some suggestion: The lecture starts with the game about finding mistakes in a proof. Continue with a small checkable proof by an AI for a proof checker. Then continue to something like a business plan, a chess game, etc. Argue that if the latter was more like the former, we could extend reward for honest/helpful behaviour and catch defection.  
* Most participants likely will have weak intuition about PSPACE and NEXP. Rather establish that the amount of human oversight gets drastically reduced by the idea of debate, and that this is crucial to make it work.  
* I think that having pro and contra argument is intuitively clearly helpful to judge the soundness of the argument, but it’s a bit divorced from the complexity theory. Ideally, find a way to bridge that more.  
* Students asked a lot about practical feasibility and experimental results. These should be more included in the curriculum.  
* Ideally, we would get a more active researcher who is currently working on debate to give the guest talk. There were a lot of questions that went unanswered.  
* Finally, the whole day might be split between Debate and Provably safe AI, which is very similar in spirit. This still has to be developed.  
  * \[Leon:\] From discussion with Stephan, this might be combined with replacing human judge by a world model

frontmatter.title: Steganography & Backdoors
frontmatter.summary: Hidden communication in LLM outputs — perfect vs computational steganography, a practical PRF-based scheme, and relations with chain-of-thought monitoring.
frontmatter.teachingGuide:
This teaching guide outlines how the materials on both steganography and backdoors were taught in-person.

| Schedule | 26.04. Steganography/Backdoors |
| :---- | ----- |
| 10:00 | **Fun exercise: The Stego Game** |
| 11:30 | Short whiteboard presentations about steganography theory and practice. We explain the difference between informational and computational indistinguishability, how the former one would require a very large random seed, while the latter can be implemented efficiently, while being secure only against computationally constrained adversaries (which is a reasonable assumption). [Steganography\_Introduction.pdf](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1hl45fOJMOE7ChJWn52JpudBLJ1lFDwGN/view?usp=drive_link) Then we go trough the basic idea behind: [Undetectable Steganography for Language models](https://openreview.net/pdf?id=fq6aQoMSHz), using pseudo-randomness to design universal scheme for LLM-based steganography. |
| 12:00 | Ask the students what they think the relevancy of steganography is for AI safety.Paper Reading (in breakout rooms if possible): [Models can hide COT with steganography](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.01926v2) [Steganography can arise and not easily prevented by paraphrasing](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2410.03768) [Model weight extractions](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2511.02620) Discussion Prompts for the papers: Why is this scenario a danger to AI safety? What are sender, receiver, adversary? What is the channel used How was the secret key exchanged? Is the steganography successful? How does it emerge? Give a small example of a possible stego-text and payload |
|  |  |
| 12:30 | **Lunch Break** |
| 13:30 |  **Discussion**: How would you plant a backdoor in an LLM? Here the students are just asked to brainstorm ideas about how to possibly plant a backdoor in an LLM, thinking about the architectural challenges. |
| 14:00 | Paper reading: [**Unelicitable Backdoors via Cryptographic Transformer Circuits**](https://arxiv.org/html/2406.02619v1) What is the advantage of their backdoor in Section 4.2 compared to Section 4.1? What are the limitations of the current approaches? How bad would this be for the worst-case interp stuff from yesterday (in the current form and in a more sophisticated setting)? |
| 14:00 | Paper Reading: [**Unelicitable Backdoors via Cryptographic Transformer Circuits**](https://arxiv.org/html/2406.02619v1) **[Cryptographic Backdoors](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.06974)** ["Backdoor Defense, Learnability and Obfuscation"](https://arxiv.org/abs/2409.03077) (Pau Christiano) ["Injecting Undetectable Backdoors in Obfuscated Neural Networks and Language Models"](https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.05660) Explaining the papers to each other What are the limitations of the current approaches? How does this relate to the worst-case interp stuff from module E.3? What is the advantage of their backdoor in Section 4.2 compared to Section 4.1? What are the properties we would like to have for a good backdoor?  **[Cryptographic Backdoors](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.06974)**  |
| 15:00 | **Exercise**: Construction of the Random ReLU two-layer network from Based on the paper: [**Shafi Goldwasser**](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.06974) et al. Calculate that this will work as intended, also there is a slight mistake in the paper that is fixed with this exercise. Potentially ask the students why the version in the paper won’t work as intended (Answer: It’s that there is no normalisation of the input vector, so just making the norm larger is already a backdoor). The exercise is broken down into intermediate steps that are simple enough for the students to go through.  [ReLU\_Exercise.pdf](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1tDucCvSSEK7wd7TJfg_PT7wF7xiArm9r/view?usp=drive_link)  |
| 16:00 | **Pause** |
| 16:30 | Presentation: **Merlin-Arthur Classifiers   [Merlin\_Arthur\_Classifiers.pdf](https://drive.google.com/file/d/13zokrgrsrUYqM9khxaPz_WD72-FMQVfP/view?usp=drive_link)** |
| 17:30 | **Debate between Claude and ChatGPT: Explain the debate format to both AIs As a topic we had “Should Frontier AI labs have to undergo external safety  So far implemented by just repeated copy-paste, one of the students built an automatic process that could be used** |
| 18:00 | Dinner |
learningOutcomes: Understand the basic idea of steganography as a technique to hide messages and that it makes CoT reasoning illegible.
Get an intuitive grasp on how it's possible to hide messages in innocent-looking text via the Stego Game, and learn the specific technique using the pseudo-random function.

Think about the steganographic setup in many different contexts and recognise sender, receiver, channel, cover distribution, message space, etc., so as to reason about questions in steganographic terms.
frontmatter.notesForFutureIterations:
* The game was a success, but it can be made harder. One way would be to make the adversary team guess which message out of four was produced by the encoding team. The encoding team knows which messages they have to hide behind when writing the cover text. This would also make it easier for the adversary to win their round, as guessing the hidden message is not really possible. Also, this would be closer to the idea of steganography. This should be set up over a channel in discord for easier time, ideally prepare beforehand if you know the participants.  
* The ReLU exercise could be made more relevant, less computational perhaps. Potentially put it into a more conceptual and less nitty-gritty form.  
* There could be a computational exercise for the Merlin-Arthur topic or generally for steganography for the tabular data with the hiring decision with a simple classifier for example. This is relatively straightforward to implement with a neural network and doesn't require transformer training.  
* Noah Topper proposes to refocus the backdoors section (D.3) to be more about CoT monitoring and alignment auditing instead.

frontmatter.title: Worst-Case Interpretability
frontmatter.summary: The limits of average-case interpretability evaluation, compact proofs as a rigorous alternative, and ARC's heuristic arguments agenda as a middle path.
frontmatter.teachingGuide:
* In practice my content filled half a day, the other should focus on more ARC stuff  
* Have a guest lecture or maybe even half a day done by someone who does a lot of ARC  
* Develop better and more difficult examples that actually use something like low-rank SVD to generate a worst-case bound, could be well as an extension of the notebook
learningOutcomes: Understand the difficulty of evaluating and validating interpretations.

Formalize interpretations as lossy compressions with quantifiable faithfulness and compactness, and identify the three sources of exponential blowup (error along paths, number of paths, input space coverage).
Articulate why worst-case bounds become vacuous even on simple models, and why this motivates an intermediate approach between worst-case and average-case evaluation.
Describe at a high level ARC's heuristic arguments agenda as a response to these limitations.
frontmatter.notesForFutureIterations:
* As I said above, having some ARC stuff that is more worked out is the way to go  
* Either have someone from ARC provide material or take more time to work through it  
* Preparing this material took me longer than anticipated and needs more attention, hence the rather compact summary/structure
